1: A type: Nadab and Abihu offer unauthorized fire — approach to YHWH outside the appointed mediator brings judgment. Christ is the only authorized way of approach: “I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me” (John 14:6). Those who reject the appointed high priest face the consuming fire rather than the blessing (Hebrews 10:26-29).
2: A type: fire from YHWH consumes the unauthorized offerers. The divine holiness that accepted the authorized sacrifice (Leviticus 9:24) now destroys those who bypass it. This is the wrath Christ absorbed in his own body for those who approach through him. “Our God is a consuming fire” (Hebrews 12:29) — those in Christ are not consumed because he absorbed the fire on their behalf.
3: A fulfillment: “through those near me I will show myself holy.” Christ is the one nearest the Father (John 1:18: “at the Father's side”) who is also perfectly holy. His holiness makes YHWH's holiness visible: “Father, glorify your name” (John 12:28). The cross is the supreme act through which the holy God is glorified before all the people.
4: A type: the bodies are removed outside the camp — judgment is expelled from the community's midst. Christ bears the judgment outside the gate (Hebrews 13:12) so that those within the covenant are not destroyed. The removal of the judged bodies enacts what the sin-offering outside the camp enacts: divine judgment is removed from the community through a representative who bears it away.
5: A type: the bodies are carried out in their priestly tunics — the office remains honorable even in judgment. Christ dies as the High Priest, in the fullness of his consecration, bearing the people's sins. His burial cloths (John 20:6-7, left folded in the tomb) testify to the completion of the priestly act — the priest laid aside his working garments because the work was fully done.
6: A type: Aaron must not mourn — the priestly vocation supersedes personal grief. Christ “endured the cross, despising the shame” (Hebrews 12:2), maintaining his priestly purposefulness in the face of death, not for his own consolation but for those he represented. He sustained his mission “for the joy that was set before him” — the priestly focus that transcends personal anguish.
7: A type: “the anointing oil of YHWH is upon you” — the consecration constrains the priest to remain at his post. Christ's anointing by the Spirit was a permanent commission that bound him to the mission until its completion. He did not descend from the cross (Matthew 27:40-42) — the anointing kept him at his post, completing what could not be interrupted.
8: A fulfillment: YHWH speaks directly to Aaron — the only direct divine address to Aaron in Leviticus. In Christ, the Father speaks to humanity directly and definitively: “in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son” (Hebrews 1:2). The new covenant, inaugurated by Christ, is characterized by direct access: “you have come to Jesus, the mediator of a new covenant” (Hebrews 12:24).
9: A type: no wine or strong drink — the priest must maintain full cognitive clarity for holy ministry. At the crucifixion, Jesus refused the wine offered to dull his pain (Matthew 27:34) — full consciousness was required for the atoning act. He would not have the boundary between holy and common blurred by compassionate sedation; he received wine only when the work was finished (John 19:30).
10: A type: “distinguish (lehavdil) between the holy and the common.” The verb havdil is the verb of creation (Genesis 1:4) — the priest's distinguishing function mirrors the divine act of separating ordered reality from chaos. Christ is the ultimate distinction: in him, the holy God approaches the common world; through faith in him, the common is made holy (1 Corinthians 6:11: “you were sanctified…in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ”).
11: A fulfillment: the priest must teach the statutes. Christ is the ultimate Teacher whose priestly authority extends to definitive interpretation of YHWH's will: the Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5-7) is the climactic priestly-teaching act of the new covenant. “You have heard that it was said… but I say to you” — Christ teaches as the priest who not only knows the law but embodies it.
12: A fulfillment: eat the grain offering at the altar — the priestly meal of consecration. The Eucharist is the NT fulfillment: “this is my body, which is given for you. Do this in remembrance of me” (Luke 22:19). The grain offering eaten by the priest in the sanctuary becomes Christ's body received by the new covenant community in the Lord's Supper.
13: A type: “it is your due and your sons' due” — the priestly portion is both a right and a gracious provision. Believers' inheritance in Christ is both a right (Romans 8:17: “heirs of God and fellow heirs with Christ”) and pure grace (Ephesians 2:8-9). The priestly inheritance — a share in the sacrificial provisions — is the type of the believer's share in everything Christ has secured.
14: A type: the priestly meal eaten in any clean place — not restricted to the sanctuary precinct. The gospel is the new covenant priestly meal received anywhere in the world by any who have been made clean through Christ. The new covenant extends the priestly meal universally: “Go therefore and make disciples of all nations” (Matthew 28:19).
15: A type: the wave offering always comes with the fat portions. In Christ, there is no separation between what belongs to God (the divine glory secured by the atonement) and what is given to his people (the priestly provision). His self-offering is simultaneously the glorification of the Father and the enrichment of the church: “for their sake I consecrate myself, that they also may be sanctified” (John 17:19).
16: A type: Moses seeks the sin offering goat with concern. Christ is the one who “came to seek and to save the lost” (Luke 19:10). The priestly zeal for proper completion of the atoning mechanism is fulfilled in Christ's relentless pursuit of those for whom his sacrifice was made: no sin offering is lost or burned without effect.
17: A fulfillment: eating the sin offering “bears the iniquity of the congregation, to make atonement for them.” This is Leviticus's most explicit statement of substitutionary mediation. Christ bears the iniquity of the many: “he himself bore our sins in his body on the tree” (1 Peter 2:24; Isaiah 53:11: “he shall bear their iniquities”). The eating-as-bearing in Leviticus 10:17 is the type; the incarnation-and-cross is the substance.
18: A type: blood not brought into the inner sanctuary means the flesh must be eaten in the outer court — outside. Christ's blood was shed outside Jerusalem (Golgotha), therefore his body is received in the world, by faith, outside the temple precinct. The gospel goes out to the world (Matthew 28:19). The priestly meal is now available wherever the gospel is proclaimed and received (Matthew 18:20).
19: A type: Aaron's explanation is accepted by Moses — the priestly judgment is received. The Father always accepts the Son's mediatorial judgment: “I knew that you always hear me” (John 11:42). Christ's priestly decisions are always accepted by the Father because he acts in perfect alignment with the Father's will. The pastoral discernment within the priestly system points forward to Christ's perfect and continuously accepted intercession.
20: A fulfillment: Moses heard and it was “acceptable (yitav) in his sight.” The Father is fully satisfied by Christ's atoning work — the NT doctrine of propitiation. Isaiah 53:11: “he shall see the travail of his soul and be satisfied.” The priestly account is in order; the work is done; the Father is pleased (Matthew 3:17: “this is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased”).
